signature abbreviation in method error sometimes hides the information you need to know why it doesn't match","user":{"login":"oxinabox","id":5127634,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjUxMjc2MzQ=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5127634?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/oxinabox","html_url":"https://github.com/oxinabox","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oxinabox/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oxinabox/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oxinabox/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oxinabox/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oxinabox/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oxinabox/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oxinabox/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oxinabox/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oxinabox/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":42281298,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw0MjI4MTI5OA==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/REPL","name":"REPL","color":"c7def8","default":false,"description":"Julia's REPL (Read Eval Print Loop)"},{"id":119746705,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxMTk3NDY3MDU=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/error%20handling","name":"error handling","color":"02d7e1","default":false,"description":"Handling of exceptions by Julia or the user"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":8,"created_at":"2023-11-10T04:32:45Z","updated_at":"2024-11-12T12:50:19Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Consider for example:
```julia
struct Foo{A, B, C} end
bar(::Foo{Tuple{Int}}) = 1
bar(::Foo{Tuple{Float64}}) = 1

bar(Foo{Tuple{Char,Int,Char,Char,Char,Char,Char,Char,Char,Char,Int, Char,Int,Char,Char,Char,Char,Char,Char,Char,Char,Int, Char,Int,Char,Char,Char,Char,Char,Char,Char,Char,Int, Char}, Dict{Int, Dict{Int,  Dict{Int,  Dict{Int,  Dict{Int,  Dict{Int,  Dict{Int,  Dict{Int, Int}}}}}}}}, Dict{Int, Dict{Int, Dict{Int, Int}}}}())
```
This is a method error becuase the first arguement is some giant tuple mixing `Char` and `Int`,
and there are only methods for if it was a 1-tuple of `Int`, or of `Float64`.
The key info you need to find what has gone wrong in your code is that first type parameter.
However, because of type abbreviation in the display of method errors, you do not always see that.
In this case the error is 
```julia
ERROR: MethodError: no method matching bar(::Foo{Tuple{…}, Dict{…}, Dict{…}})

Closest candidates are:
  bar(::Foo{Tuple{Float64}})
   @ Main REPL[3]:1
  bar(::Foo{Tuple{Int64}})
   @ Main REPL[2]:1
```
We can't tell what is wrong with the `Tuple` type param, since its parameters are hidden.
(`show(err)` does make this display fully as you would hope. in my real world example though `show(err)` resulted in enough text being displayed from my stacktrace that my terminal started glitching)


As I understand it, the logic in type-signature abbreviation is such that any type parameters that are required to hit the particular dispatch are shown.
That makes sense for display in stacktraces -- you can know why a particular path was followed.
But that logic does not work for `MethodError`. For method error we want to see enough info to know why no existing method was hit.

I propose that, for now, we should not abbreviate type signatures in method errors,
until we can work out suitable logic for their abbreviation.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52113/reactions","total_count":6,"+1":6,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52113/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2470450338","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52113#issuecomment-2470450338","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52113","id":2470450338,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6TQBSi","user":{"login":"rafaqz","id":2534009,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI1MzQwMDk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2534009?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz","html_url":"https://github.com/rafaqz","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-11-12T12:50:18Z","updated_at":"2024-11-12T12:50:18Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Here's another one from the wild:

```julia
Possible fix, define
  similar(::Type{T}, ::Tuple{Union{…}, Union{…}, Vararg{…}}) where T<:AbstractArray
```

From this issue:
https://github.com/rafaqz/DimensionalData.jl/pull/852

In this case I would have to ask op to repost the error from `err` or run it myself, adding a small but real barrier to understanding the problem. seriously","user":{"login":"CameronBieganek","id":8310743,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgzMTA3NDM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8310743?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek","html_url":"https://github.com/CameronBieganek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/CameronBieganek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":115619,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxMTU2MTk=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/domain:docs","name":"domain:docs","color":"02d7e1","default":false,"description":"This documentation"},{"id":522769435,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw1MjI3Njk0MzU=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/domain:collections","name":"domain:collections","color":"09aeb1","default":false,"description":"Data structures holding multiple items, e.g. sets"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-01-02T22:50:19Z","updated_at":"2024-07-15T14:16:13Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"This will probably get closed as a duplicate, but I think it is high time that we provide actual interface definitions for the various collection categories listed on the ["Collections and Data Structures"](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/base/collections/) page of the manual. That page hints at interfaces for the various categories, but does not provide a clear guide to which methods are required and which methods are optional in order to implement the various interfaces. (Plus, that page includes docstrings for a bunch of functions that most likely do *not* need to be implemented by new types.)

Here is a list of collection interfaces from that page that need better documentation on the interface requirements:

- [ ] General Collections
- [ ] Indexable Collections
- [ ] Dictionaries (`AbstractDict`)
- [ ] Set-Like Collections (`AbstractSet`)
- [ ] Dequeues

See also #34677 and #25941. And also #28784 for documentation of the `AbstractString` interface.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52705/reactions","total_count":4,"+1":4,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52705/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2228613622","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52705#issuecomment-2228613622","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52705","id":2228613622,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6E1fH2","user":{"login":"rafaqz","id":2534009,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI1MzQwMDk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2534009?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz","html_url":"https://github.com/rafaqz","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-07-15T14:16:12Z","updated_at":"2024-07-15T14:16:12Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"This should be kept open rather than distributed across multiple smaller issues, as that hasn't seemed to work so far.

For reference I've started fleshing out interface tests for some of these at https://github.com/rafaqz/Interfaces.jl/tree/main/BaseInterfaces 
There is some interest in getting BaseInterfaces.jl registered so people can test their objects against these interfaces (https://github.com/rafaqz/Interfaces.jl/issues/48) so it would be good to collaborate these things.

Interfaces.jl syntax with descriptions connected to test functions makes the interface very clear, and will let us test their assertions against all base objects to see if they actually hold. That workflow is already set up and working with the current tests. 

`AbstractSet` is well tested and documented because of the @oxinabox issue, `AbstractArray` and iteration because of the available docs, but the others much less so.

Unfortunately Base breaks its own documented interfaces often enough that it's still not totally straightforward, like `LogicalIndex` not defining `getindex!` as an `AbstractArray`. But we can at least blacklist those objects in some kind of "table of shame" when its all done explicitly.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2228613622/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

- If a version is actually dangerous to use, it should be yanked
- Like it has a security vulnerability or someone accidentally committed an rm -rf / call
- “Oops, I released a version with a bug” is not a reason

And in response to: "But the readmes for general registry and ColPrac still both say to yank if you register a version with too wide compat bounds"

- No, that’s not right. You should just fix the compat bounds in the registry
- And ideally, also make a release that has the right bounds in the project file
- Largely just because it’s confusing when those don’t match","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/ColPrac/issues/comments/2096658410/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/ColPrac/pulls/comments/1591412465","pull_request_review_id":2041434980,"id":1591412465,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOEFNQ485e2wbx","diff_hunk":"@@ -198,7 +198,13 @@ Simply releasing a patch for PackageA (v1.1.1) that removes support for v0.4 won
 It might seem sufficient to just pin the downstream packages to use v1.0.0, but there may be a lot of them to fix, and you can't be certain you're aware of them all.
 It also does nothing to prevent new compatibility issues from arising in the future.
 
-To fix this, you should still release a patch of PackageA (v1.1.1) that removes support for v0.4 of PackageB, but you should then mark v1.1.0 of PackageA as broken in the registry.
+To fix this, you should still release a patch of PackageA (v1.1.1) that removes support for v0.4 of PackageB, and also remove the compatability from
+Compat.toml for the package in the general registry. This should require changing two compat bounds - removing the julia version from the package 
+version that will not work with it, and adding the package version to the julia version it is in fact compatible with.
+

working"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2024-03-05T13:15:12Z","updated_at":"2024-04-18T16:44:01Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"**Describe the bug 🐞**

When optimizing with `NelderMead()` things work completely fine unless I use `lb` and `ub` keywords. 
When I try to enforce bounds (between zero and one for all parameters) Optimizatin.jl for some reason calls `grad!` some way into the optimization, but it looks like `cache.f.grad` here is `nothing` for `NelderMead`:
https://github.com/SciML/Optimization.jl/blob/038c7b6bd34111cd4a2fdb74886bf7c05a026c30/lib/OptimizationOptimJL/src/OptimizationOptimJL.jl#L291

I'm not sure why `grad!` is called on a gradient free method.

**Expected behavior**

The optimisation should run as normal with `lb`/`ub` args

**Minimal Reproducible Example 👇**

I have a complicated simulation behind the objective function, and the error only comes half way through optimizing it. So, not sure how to make an MWE. I'm not sure how this can happen or what is triggering it.

But this is essentially it, without the exact objective func:

```julia
ub = fill(Float32(1.0), 35)
lb = fill(Float32(0.0), 35)
x0 = rand(Float32, 35)
prob = OptimizationProblem(objective_func, x0, p; lb, ub)
@time sol = solve(prob, NelderMead())
```

the bug 🐞**

When optimizing with `NelderMead()` things work completely fine unless I use `lb` and `ub` keywords. 
When I try to enforce bounds (between zero and one for all parameters) Optimizatin.jl for some reason calls `grad!` some way into the optimization, but it looks like `cache.f.grad` here is `nothing` for `NelderMead`:
https://github.com/SciML/Optimization.jl/blob/038c7b6bd34111cd4a2fdb74886bf7c05a026c30/lib/OptimizationOptimJL/src/OptimizationOptimJL.jl#L291

I'm not sure why `grad!` is called on a gradient free method.

**Expected behavior**

The optimisation should run as normal with `lb`/`ub` args

**Minimal Reproducible Example 👇**

I have a complicated simulation behind the objective function, and the error only comes half way through optimizing it. So, not sure how to make an MWE. I'm not sure how this can happen or what is triggering it.

But this is essentially it, without the exact objective func:

```julia
ub = fill(Float32(1.0), 35)
lb = fill(Float32(0.0), 35)
x0 = rand(Float32, 35)
prob = OptimizationProblem(objective_func, x0, p; lb, ub)
@time sol = solve(prob, NelderMead())
```

the bug 🐞**

When optimizing with `NelderMead()` things work completely fine unless I use `lb` and `ub` keywords. 
When I try to enforce bounds (between zero and one for all parameters) Optimizatin.jl for some reason calls `grad!` some way into the optimization, but it looks like `cache.f.grad` here is `nothing` for `NelderMead`:
https://github.com/SciML/Optimization.jl/blob/038c7b6bd34111cd4a2fdb74886bf7c05a026c30/lib/OptimizationOptimJL/src/OptimizationOptimJL.jl#L291

I'm not sure why `grad!` is called on a gradient free method.

**Expected behavior**

The optimisation should run as normal with `lb`/`ub` args

**Minimal Reproducible Example 👇**

I have a complicated simulation behind the objective function, and the error only comes half way through optimizing it. So, not sure how to make an MWE. I'm not sure how this can happen or what is triggering it.

But this is essentially it, without the exact objective func:

```julia
ub = fill(Float32(1.0), 35)
lb = fill(Float32(0.0), 35)
x0 = rand(Float32, 35)
prob = OptimizationProblem(objective_func, x0, p; lb, ub)
@time sol = solve(prob, NelderMead())
```

the bug 🐞**

When optimizing with `NelderMead()` things work completely fine unless I use `lb` and `ub` keywords. 
When I try to enforce bounds (between zero and one for all parameters) Optimizatin.jl for some reason calls `grad!` some way into the optimization, but it looks like `cache.f.grad` here is `nothing` for `NelderMead`:
https://github.com/SciML/Optimization.jl/blob/038c7b6bd34111cd4a2fdb74886bf7c05a026c30/lib/OptimizationOptimJL/src/OptimizationOptimJL.jl#L291

I'm not sure why `grad!` is called on a gradient free method.

**Expected behavior**

The optimisation should run as normal with `lb`/`ub` args

**Minimal Reproducible Example 👇**

I have a complicated simulation behind the objective function, and the error only comes half way through optimizing it. So, not sure how to make an MWE. I'm not sure how this can happen or what is triggering it.

But this is essentially it, without the exact objective func:

```julia
ub = fill(Float32(1.0), 35)
lb = fill(Float32(0.0), 35)
x0 = rand(Float32, 35)
prob = OptimizationProblem(objective_func, x0, p; lb, ub)
@time sol = solve(prob, NelderMead())
```

the bug 🐞**

When optimizing with `NelderMead()` things work completely fine unless I use `lb` and `ub` keywords. 
When I try to enforce bounds (between zero and one for all parameters) Optimizatin.jl for some reason calls `grad!` some way into the optimization, but it looks like `cache.f.grad` here is `nothing` for `NelderMead`:
https://github.com/SciML/Optimization.jl/blob/038c7b6bd34111cd4a2fdb74886bf7c05a026c30/lib/OptimizationOptimJL/src/OptimizationOptimJL.jl#L291

I'm not sure why `grad!` is called on a gradient free method.

**Expected behavior**

The optimisation should run as normal with `lb`/`ub` args

**Minimal Reproducible Example 👇**

I have a complicated simulation behind the objective function, and the error only comes half way through optimizing it. So, not sure how to make an MWE. I'm not sure how this can happen or what is triggering it.

But this is essentially it, without the exact objective func:

```julia
ub = fill(Float32(1.0), 35)
lb = fill(Float32(0.0), 35)
x0 = rand(Float32, 35)
prob = OptimizationProblem(objective_func, x0, p; lb, ub)
@time sol = solve(prob, NelderMead())
```

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53188","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53188/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53188/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53188/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53188","id":2118749484,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5-SY0s","number":53188,"title":"Add gerneric function for lazy `PermutedDarrays` or `permutedims` keyword","user":{"login":"rafaqz","id":2534009,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI1MzQwMDk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2534009?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz","html_url":"https://github.com/rafaqz","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-02-05T14:52:30Z","updated_at":"2024-02-05T14:52:30Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Currently we need to call the the constructor `PermutedDimsArray` to get non-allocating `permutedims` behaviour. This means we are not free to adjust the interface - we really need to return an actual `PermutedDimsArray`.

This causes some problems. For example, in DiskArrays.jl we have a custom [`PermutedDiskArray`](https://github.com/meggart/DiskArrays.jl/blob/main/src/permute.jl) generated by `permutedims`, and its lazy. But we cant return this from `PermutedDimsArray` without doing _bad things_ in the `PermutedDimsArray` constructor.

DimensionalData.jl is the most common package wrapping a DiskArrays.jl objects (in much of the spatial data sciences) and there we use `PermutedDimsArray` as a low-cost optimisation for permuting in-memory arrays. But this of course doesn't work on DiskArrays.jl as the disk array is no longer visible through the `PermutedDimsArray` wrapper without special-casing.

So I'm proposing 2 possible approaches:
1. A new method like `lazypermutedims` or similar that by default calls `PermutedDimsArray` but we are free to extend however we need to
2. Add a keyword to `permuteddims` of `lazy=false`, and `true` calls `PermutedDimsArray`. This might have some type instability issues.

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53188","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53188/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53188/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53188/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53188","id":2118749484,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5-SY0s","number":53188,"title":"Add gerneric function for lazy `PermutedDimsArray` or `permutedims` keyword","user":{"login":"rafaqz","id":2534009,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI1MzQwMDk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2534009?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz","html_url":"https://github.com/rafaqz","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2024-02-05T14:52:30Z","updated_at":"2024-02-06T09:46:14Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Currently we need to call the the constructor `PermutedDimsArray` to get non-allocating `permutedims` behaviour. This means we are not free to adjust the interface - we really need to return an actual `PermutedDimsArray`.

This causes some problems. For example, in DiskArrays.jl we have a custom [`PermutedDiskArray`](https://github.com/meggart/DiskArrays.jl/blob/main/src/permute.jl) generated by `permutedims`, and its lazy. But we cant return this from `PermutedDimsArray` without doing _bad things_ in the `PermutedDimsArray` constructor.

DimensionalData.jl is the most common package wrapping a DiskArrays.jl objects (in much of the spatial data sciences) and there we use `PermutedDimsArray` as a low-cost optimisation for permuting in-memory arrays. But this of course doesn't work on DiskArrays.jl as the disk array is no longer visible through the `PermutedDimsArray` wrapper without special-casing.

So I'm proposing 2 possible approaches:
1. A new method like `lazypermutedims` or similar that by default calls `PermutedDimsArray` but we are free to extend however we need to
2. Add a keyword to `permuteddims` of `lazy=false`, and `true` calls `PermutedDimsArray`. This might have some type instability issues.

1. A new method like `lazypermutedims` or similar that by default calls `PermutedDimsArray` but we are free to extend however we need to
2. Add a keyword to `permuteddims` of `lazy=false`, and `true` calls `PermutedDimsArray`. This might have some type instability issues.

`view` solves our problem because we are allowed to return a `SubDiskArray` from it that behaves very much like a `SubArray`, but lazily and without `show` printing.

We dont need the old type because `SubDiskArray` implements the behaviours itself.

`reshape` is the same - we are allowed to return `ReshapedDiskArray`. 

But `PermutedDimsArray` we have to return a `PermutedDimsArray`. Thats a problem because we dont own `show` for `PermutedDimsArray` and showing a GB from the cloud is not a good thing to do to users.

The general idea of using these wrapper types directly rather than functions that can return more than one type seems fragile to me.

This means we are not free to adjust the interface - we really need to return an actual `PermutedDimsArray`.

This causes some problems. For example, in DiskArrays.jl we have a custom [`PermutedDiskArray`](https://github.com/meggart/DiskArrays.jl/blob/main/src/permute.jl) generated by `permutedims`, and its lazy. But we cant return this from `PermutedDimsArray` without doing _bad things_ in the `PermutedDimsArray` constructor.

DimensionalData.jl is the most common package wrapping a DiskArrays.jl objects (in much of the spatial data sciences) and there we use `PermutedDimsArray` as a low-cost optimisation for permuting in-memory arrays. But this of course doesn't work on DiskArrays.jl as the disk array is no longer visible through the `PermutedDimsArray` wrapper without special-casing.

So I'm proposing 2 possible approaches:
1. A new method like `lazypermutedims` or similar that by default calls `PermutedDimsArray` but we are free to extend however we need to
2. Add a keyword to `permuteddims` of `lazy=false`, and `true` calls `PermutedDimsArray`. This might have some type instability issues.

In this example the key information about what causes the method error is lost due to type symplification with `...`.

I really want to know what is in `Val{...}`:



```julia
julia> DynamicGrids.descendable(sd)
ERROR: MethodError: no method matching _demo(::DynamicGrids.Descend, ::DynamicGrids.RuleData{…}, ::SetNeighbors{…}, ::Val{…})

Closest candidates are:
  _demo(::DynamicGrids.DemoMode, ::DynamicGrids.RuleData{<:Any, N}, ::Any, ::Val{<:CellRule}) where N
   @ DynamicGrids ~/.julia/dev/DynamicGrids/src/utils.jl:72
  _demo(::DynamicGrids.DemoMode, ::DynamicGrids.RuleData{<:Any, N}, ::Any, ::Val{<:SetCellRule}) where N

handling","color":"02d7e1","default":false,"description":"Handling of exceptions by Julia or the user"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2023-11-10T04:32:45Z","updated_at":"2024-01-04T18:02:30Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Consider for example:
```julia
struct Foo{A, B, C} end
bar(::Foo{Tuple{Int}}) = 1
bar(::Foo{Tuple{Float64}}) = 1

bar(Foo{Tuple{Char,Int,Char,Char,Char,Char,Char,Char,Char,Char,Int, Char,Int,Char,Char,Char,Char,Char,Char,Char,Char,Int, Char,Int,Char,Char,Char,Char,Char,Char,Char,Char,Int, Char}, Dict{Int, Dict{Int,  Dict{Int,  Dict{Int,  Dict{Int,  Dict{Int,  Dict{Int,  Dict{Int, Int}}}}}}}}, Dict{Int, Dict{Int, Dict{Int, Int}}}}())
```
This is a method error becuase the first arguement is some giant tuple mixing `Char` and `Int`,
and there are only methods for if it was a 1-tuple of `Int`, or of `Float64`.
The key info you need to find what has gone wrong in your code is that first type parameter.
However, because of type abbreviation in the display of method errors, you do not always see that.
In this case the error is 
```julia
ERROR: MethodError: no method matching bar(::Foo{Tuple{…}, Dict{…}, Dict{…}})

Closest candidates are:
  bar(::Foo{Tuple{Float64}})
   @ Main REPL[3]:1
  bar(::Foo{Tuple{Int64}})
   @ Main REPL[2]:1
```
We can't tell what is wrong with the `Tuple` type param, since its parameters are hidden.
(`show(err)` does make this display fully as you would hope. in my real world example though `show(err)` resulted in enough text being displayed from my stacktrace that my terminal started glitching)


As I understand it, the logic in type-signature abbreviation is such that any type parameters that are required to hit the particular dispatch are shown.
That makes sense for display in stacktraces -- you can know why a particular path was followed.
But that logic does not work for `MethodError`. For method error we want to see enough info to know why no existing method was hit.

I propose that, for now, we should not abbreviate type signatures in method errors,
until we can work out suitable logic for their abbreviation.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52113/reactions","total_count":6,"+1":6,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52113/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1877536616","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52113#issuecomment-1877536616","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52113","id":1877536616,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5v6O9o","user":{"login":"rafaqz","id":2534009,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI1MzQwMDk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2534009?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz","html_url":"https://github.com/rafaqz","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-01-04T18:02:29Z","updated_at":"2024-01-04T18:02:29Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"I just hit this too. The former is a hash, the latter is much quicker and easier to compute.~~

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments/1347856330","pull_request_review_id":1660524845,"id":1347856330,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABkWpM5QVqfK","diff_hunk":"@@ -1522,14 +1522,33 @@ _isdisjoint(as::Tuple, bs::Tuple) = !(as[1] in bs) && _isdisjoint(tail(as), bs)
 """
     Base.dataids(A::AbstractArray)
 
-Return a tuple of `UInt`s that represent the mutable data segments of an array.
+Return a tuple of `UInt`s that identify the mutable data segments of an array.
 
-Custom arrays that would like to opt-in to aliasing detection of their component
-parts can specialize this method to return the concatenation of the `dataids` of
-their component parts.  A typical definition for an array that wraps a parent is
-`Base.dataids(C::CustomArray) = dataids(C.parent)`.
+These values are used to determine if two arrays might share memory with [`Base.mightalias`](@ref).
+The default implementation recursively combines the `dataids` of all fields of the struct.
+
+Custom arrays only need to implement a custom `dataids` method if:
+
+* they wish to ignore some fields (with non-empty `dataids`) in aliasing considerations;
+    for example this can be the case if an array is used to store intentionally-shared
+    metadata or other data that is not mutated by `setindex!`
+
+* or they depend upon non-array fields (with empty `dataids`) to define their indexable
+    contents that they wish to include in aliasing considerations.
 """
-dataids(A::AbstractArray) = (UInt(objectid(A)),)
+function dataids(A::AbstractArray)
+    @inline
+    if @generated
+        :(ids = tuple($([:(dataids(getfield(A, $i))...) for i in 1:fieldcount(A)]...)))
+    else
+        ids = _splatmap(dataids, ntuple(i -> getfield(A, i), {"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments/1347881602","pull_request_review_id":1660572045,"id":1347881602,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABkWpM5QVwqC","diff_hunk":"@@ -1522,14 +1522,33 @@ _isdisjoint(as::Tuple, bs::Tuple) = !(as[1] in bs) && _isdisjoint(tail(as), bs)
 """
     Base.dataids(A::AbstractArray)
 
-Return a tuple of `UInt`s that represent the mutable data segments of an array.
+Return a tuple of `UInt`s that identify the mutable data segments of an array.
 
-Custom arrays that would like to opt-in to aliasing detection of their component
-parts can specialize this method to return the concatenation of the `dataids` of
-their component parts.  A typical definition for an array that wraps a parent is
-`Base.dataids(C::CustomArray) = dataids(C.parent)`.
+These values are used to determine if two arrays might share memory with [`Base.mightalias`](@ref).
+The default implementation recursively combines the `dataids` of all fields of the struct.
+
+Custom arrays only need to implement a custom `dataids` method if:
+
+* they wish to ignore some fields (with non-empty `dataids`) in aliasing considerations;
+    for example this can be the case if an array is used to store intentionally-shared
+    metadata or other data that is not mutated by `setindex!`
+
+* or they depend upon non-array fields (with empty `dataids`) to define their indexable
+    contents that they wish to include in aliasing considerations.
 """
-dataids(A::AbstractArray) = (UInt(objectid(A)),)
+function dataids(A::AbstractArray)
+    @inline
+    if @generated
+        :(ids = tuple($([:(dataids(getfield(A, $i))...) for i in 1:fieldcount(A)]...)))
+    else
+        ids = _splatmap(dataids, ntuple(i -> getfield(A, i), Val(nfields(A))))","path":"base/abstractarray.jl","commit_id":"c5d6f31a2a9b88f114897f3d78a07d7096e2413a","original_commit_id":"c5d6f31a2a9b88f114897f3d78a07d7096e2413a","user":{"login":"rafaqz","id":2534009,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI1MzQwMDk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2534009?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz","html_url":"https://github.com/rafaqz","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Haha maybe its literally only `Tridiagonal`.

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments/1347898725","pull_request_review_id":1660598986,"id":1347898725,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABkWpM5QV01l","diff_hunk":"@@ -1522,14 +1522,33 @@ _isdisjoint(as::Tuple, bs::Tuple) = !(as[1] in bs) && _isdisjoint(tail(as), bs)
 """
     Base.dataids(A::AbstractArray)
 
-Return a tuple of `UInt`s that represent the mutable data segments of an array.
+Return a tuple of `UInt`s that identify the mutable data segments of an array.
 
-Custom arrays that would like to opt-in to aliasing detection of their component
-parts can specialize this method to return the concatenation of the `dataids` of
-their component parts.  A typical definition for an array that wraps a parent is
-`Base.dataids(C::CustomArray) = dataids(C.parent)`.
+These values are used to determine if two arrays might share memory with [`Base.mightalias`](@ref).
+The default implementation recursively combines the `dataids` of all fields of the struct.
+
+Custom arrays only need to implement a custom `dataids` method if:
+
+* they wish to ignore some fields (with non-empty `dataids`) in aliasing considerations;
+    for example this can be the case if an array is used to store intentionally-shared
+    metadata or other data that is not mutated by `setindex!`
+
+* or they depend upon non-array fields (with empty `dataids`) to define their indexable
+    contents that they wish to include in aliasing considerations.
 """
-dataids(A::AbstractArray) = (UInt(objectid(A)),)
+function dataids(A::AbstractArray)
+    @inline
+    if @generated
+        :(ids = tuple($([:(dataids(getfield(A, $i))...) for i in 1:fieldcount(A)]...)))
+    else
+        ids = _splatmap(dataids, ntuple(i -> getfield(A, i), content is hard to deal with. This contribution aims to drastically improve that.

Closes #41791, closes #41435, and I think also resolves #40228 and resolves #28690.

## Current holdups

- Making CI pass

## New public API

- `TaggedString`
- `TaggedChar`
- `taggedstring`
- `Base.Face`
- `addface!`
- `@S_str`

## Motivation

Julia already treats the REPL experience better than most other languages out there (🐍), and I think it's great how often I see the REPL as a selling point of Julia.

However... styling is hard. Across `Base` and the package ecosystem we have a painful mixture of raw ANSI codes and an unsightly pile of `printstyled` lines. This isn't just ugly, this leads to reoccurring issues with incorrectly computed string lengths/padding and unterminated styling codes (e.g. #37568 and #45521).

We can do better than this. Rethinking our approach to styled content allows us to do away with this class of bugs/headaches altogether, and gain a number of shiny new features at the same time.

Hy hope is that the capabilities introduced by this PR will lead to a much more robust approach to string styling, and by making it easier to produce well-styled content even more beautiful REPL experiences across the board.

## Rundown of changes

An `StyledString` type is introduced that effectively sits on top of other string types. It wraps another `AbstractString` but then separately stores a list of attributes applied to particular regions of the wrapped string. Regions are represented by a `UnitRange{Int}`, and attributes as a `Pair{Symbol, Any}`.

This effectively creates "content" and "attributes" layers, allowing both to be handled much better (conveniently and robustly) than when mixed together. Attributes can be *`Any`thing*, which allows for more than just styling information, e.g. hyperlinks, or other data which makes sense to attach to a region of a string like source location information. Really `StyledString` should be called `PropertizedString`, but I think that's much less catchy 😛.

An `StyledChar` type is also added, which is a mirror of `AbstractString`, just wrapping an `AbstractChar` instead.

To handle styling information, a new type is added to contain all possibly relevant styling information — `Face`. This is very much inspired by https://www.gnu.org/software/emacs/manual/html_node/elisp/Faces.html, as I have found this system to work rather well in practice (why invent a new approach when there's a lovely battle-tested one we can be inspired by? 😉).

The main way faces work is by having a global `Dics{Symbol, Face}` of face names. This has a number of advantages:
- It makes it easy to reference faces by their name
- Packages can easily add new faces
- It makes it possible for faces to inherit from other faces (e.g. have a `:julia_help_prompt` face that inherits from `:julia_prompt`)

Conversion functions to accept and load face specifications from a `Dict{String, Any}` are also added. By loading `~/.julia/config/faces.toml` this resolves #41435.

To handle printing faces well, we need to inquire about the terminal a fair bit more, and so I've replaced `tcap` with a terminfo parser implemented in accordance with `term(5)`.

Lastly, we have a string macro (`@S_str`) that was rather nasty to write, but is very convenient.

#### Currently unimplemented

- `replace`
- property removal

## Usage examples

The example from the `@S_str` docs

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235492934-d3299633-36fe-4a21-8321-83e24d8c4a6f.png)

Easy nested styling

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235493196-753f50e4-3acc-4821-9146-8c1aef5e6cd1.png)

No more issues with unterminated ANSI codes

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235494848-eccefbda-f943-4c1d-85a0-dbd2297b9217.png)

String functions operate on the unstyled content, while preserving styling where possible

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235496767-1d537db9-befc-4ee6-b482-e91778e32ceb.png)

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235496290-d3b6bb79-9c33-4dce-bf6f-b20d696cc5b2.png)

Styled text will actually automatically fall back to the un-styled form when printed to an `io` with `:color != true`.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235496566-330a621d-e0d9-4b36-8714-e9d225e89ef7.png)

Incrementally styling a string, and changing face definitions on-the-fly (note: this uses currently "private API" functions)

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235499614-ce8d5aca-a561-4280-979e-d6c176efd1df.png)

## Fancier usage examples

The result of the example "banner using `S"..."`" commit

![image](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/assets/20903656/1e5cdf8a-4e3e-48ef-ae94-a0850c484270)

A fancier `^R` implementation, that's been implemented using `StyledString`s and `JuliaSyntax` that would be a massive pain to do via `printstyled` et al.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235492830-4e0476d1-0716-48df-be8a-b1f26fe9b53d.png)

With very little work, docstring previews could look like this

![image](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/assets/20903656/f546de5c-e2ec-4ba8-bcf7-d2ff714bb928)

merge"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":56,"created_at":"2023-05-01T18:01:45Z","updated_at":"2023-09-03T12:46:31Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/49586","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/49586","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/49586.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/49586.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Styled content is hard to deal with. This contribution aims to drastically improve that.

## Current holdups

- Lack of feedback
- Lack of documentation
- Lack of tests

## New public API

- `StyledString`
- `StyledChar`
- `Base.Face`
- `addface!`
- `@S_str`
- `styledstring`

should anything else be added?

## Motivation

Julia already treats the REPL experience better than most other languages out there (🐍), and I think it's great how often I see the REPL as a selling point of Julia.

However... styling is hard. Across `Base` and the package ecosystem we have a painful mixture of raw ANSI codes and an unsightly pile of `printstyled` lines. This isn't just ugly, this leads to reoccurring issues with incorrectly computed string lengths/padding and unterminated styling codes (e.g. #37568 and #45521).

We can do better than this. Rethinking our approach to styled content allows us to do away with this class of bugs/headaches altogether, and gain a number of shiny new features at the same time.

Hy hope is that the capabilities introduced by this PR will lead to a much more robust approach to string styling, and by making it easier to produce well-styled content even more beautiful REPL experiences across the board.

## Rundown of changes

An `StyledString` type is introduced that effectively sits on top of other string types. It wraps another `AbstractString` but then separately stores a list of attributes applied to particular regions of the wrapped string. Regions are represented by a `UnitRange{Int}`, and attributes as a `Pair{Symbol, Any}`.

This effectively creates "content" and "attributes" layers, allowing both to be handled much better (conveniently and robustly) than when mixed together. Attributes can be *`Any`thing*, which allows for more than just styling information, e.g. hyperlinks, or other data which makes sense to attach to a region of a string like source location information. Really `StyledString` should be called `PropertizedString`, but I think that's much less catchy 😛.

An `StyledChar` type is also added, which is a mirror of `AbstractString`, just wrapping an `AbstractChar` instead.

To handle styling information, a new type is added to contain all possibly relevant styling information — `Face`. This is very much inspired by https://www.gnu.org/software/emacs/manual/html_node/elisp/Faces.html, as I have found this system to work rather well in practice (why invent a new approach when there's a lovely battle-tested one we can be inspired by? 😉).

The main way faces work is by having a global `Dics{Symbol, Face}` of face names. This has a number of advantages:
- It makes it easy to reference faces by their name
- Packages can easily add new faces
- It makes it possible for faces to inherit from other faces (e.g. have a `:julia_help_prompt` face that inherits from `:julia_prompt`)

Conversion functions to accept and load face specifications from a `Dict{String, Any}` are also added. By loading `~/.julia/config/faces.toml` this resolves #41435.

To handle printing faces well, we need to inquire about the terminal a fair bit more, and so I've replaced `tcap` with a terminfo parser implemented in accordance with `term(5)`.

Lastly, we have a string macro (`@S_str`) that was rather nasty to write, but is very convenient.

#### Currently unimplemented

- `replace`
- property removal

## Usage examples

The example from the `@S_str` docs

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235492934-d3299633-36fe-4a21-8321-83e24d8c4a6f.png)

Easy nested styling

content is hard to deal with. This contribution aims to drastically improve that.

Closes #41791, closes #41435, and I think also resolves #40228 and resolves #28690.

## Current holdups

- Review and acceptance

## New public API

- `StyledString`
- `StyledChar`
- `Base.Face`
- `addface!`
- `@S_str`
- `styledstring`

should anything else be added?

## Motivation

Julia already treats the REPL experience better than most other languages out there (🐍), and I think it's great how often I see the REPL as a selling point of Julia.

However... styling is hard. Across `Base` and the package ecosystem we have a painful mixture of raw ANSI codes and an unsightly pile of `printstyled` lines. This isn't just ugly, this leads to reoccurring issues with incorrectly computed string lengths/padding and unterminated styling codes (e.g. #37568 and #45521).

We can do better than this. Rethinking our approach to styled content allows us to do away with this class of bugs/headaches altogether, and gain a number of shiny new features at the same time.

Hy hope is that the capabilities introduced by this PR will lead to a much more robust approach to string styling, and by making it easier to produce well-styled content even more beautiful REPL experiences across the board.

## Rundown of changes

An `StyledString` type is introduced that effectively sits on top of other string types. It wraps another `AbstractString` but then separately stores a list of attributes applied to particular regions of the wrapped string. Regions are represented by a `UnitRange{Int}`, and attributes as a `Pair{Symbol, Any}`.

This effectively creates "content" and "attributes" layers, allowing both to be handled much better (conveniently and robustly) than when mixed together. Attributes can be *`Any`thing*, which allows for more than just styling information, e.g. hyperlinks, or other data which makes sense to attach to a region of a string like source location information. Really `StyledString` should be called `PropertizedString`, but I think that's much less catchy 😛.

An `StyledChar` type is also added, which is a mirror of `AbstractString`, just wrapping an `AbstractChar` instead.

To handle styling information, a new type is added to contain all possibly relevant styling information — `Face`. This is very much inspired by https://www.gnu.org/software/emacs/manual/html_node/elisp/Faces.html, as I have found this system to work rather well in practice (why invent a new approach when there's a lovely battle-tested one we can be inspired by? 😉).

The main way faces work is by having a global `Dics{Symbol, Face}` of face names. This has a number of advantages:
- It makes it easy to reference faces by their name
- Packages can easily add new faces
- It makes it possible for faces to inherit from other faces (e.g. have a `:julia_help_prompt` face that inherits from `:julia_prompt`)

Conversion functions to accept and load face specifications from a `Dict{String, Any}` are also added. By loading `~/.julia/config/faces.toml` this resolves #41435.

To handle printing faces well, we need to inquire about the terminal a fair bit more, and so I've replaced `tcap` with a terminfo parser implemented in accordance with `term(5)`.

Lastly, we have a string macro (`@S_str`) that was rather nasty to write, but is very convenient.

#### Currently unimplemented

- `replace`
- property removal

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235496566-330a621d-e0d9-4b36-8714-e9d225e89ef7.png)

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51071","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51071/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51071/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51071/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51071","id":1868555586,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5vX-VC","number":51071,"title":"`LogicalIndex` should not be `<: AbstractArray`","user":{"login":"rafaqz","id":2534009,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI1MzQwMDk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2534009?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz","html_url":"https://github.com/rafaqz","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2023-08-27T16:35:52Z","updated_at":"2023-08-27T16:35:52Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Having `AbstractArray` around that don't define `getindex` is problematic for anyone extending the Array interface or dealing with array indexing. 

The Julia interface documentation [specifies that all arrays need `size` and  `getindex`](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/manual/interfaces/#man-interface-array). But in practice it seems even `getindex` is not needed. `LogicalIndex` not defining `getindex` also makes it ok to do in the ecosystem, so packages like InvertedIndices.jl do it to.

Really, `LogicalIndex` is an iterable, but not an `AbstractArray`, and we should mark that somehow in the type.

I guess this is one for julia 2.0","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51071/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51071/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

NamedTuples support the interfaces of both structs and tuples. But neither structs nor tuples support indexing by symbol. It may be convenient for situations with a non-identifier property name, but it mixes the semantics of `getproperty` and `getindex` confusingly. 

```jl
julia> NamedTuple([Symbol("2") => 20, :a => 10])[Symbol("2")]
20

julia> NamedTuple([Symbol("2") => 20, :a => 10])[2]
10
```

`getproperty` should not decide its semantics based on the type of the argument --- for different behavior, use a different function. Imho, `getindex(::NamedTuple, i)` should index _positions_ and `getproperty` should go by property name. These should be kept separate. I was imagining in that case we could swap out all the gensyms to the new function where that is cached. Maybe that's hard. But it would be a major responsiveness improvement in the REPL.

But it would be nice if the constructors could of DimArray and DenseAxisArray and e.g. KeyedArray from AxisKeys.jl could all get the lookups from the other array type, there is no reason to pass in the lookup to `DimArray` if the parent array already has them.

on the discussions with @nalimilan this PR proposes:
* to add a contract to `Ordered` trait so that external packages can also use it
* add `Ordered` trait to relevant exported types that define `isless` methods in a safe way

From this PR I have excluded the following types defined in standard Julia installation:
* `AbstractArray`
* `Tuple`
* `NamedTuple`
* `Pair`
* `Ptr`
* `VersionNumber`
* `Base.CoreLogging.LogLevel`
* `Base.Enums.Enum`
* `Base.SHA1`
* `Base.Unicode.GraphemeIterator`
* `Base.UUID`
* `Pkg.Resolve.FieldValue`
* `Pkg.Resolve.VersionWeight`
* `Sockets.IPAddr`

as I thought that either very rare or do not meet the contract I proposed (like `AbstractArray`)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/37239/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/37239/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1215345136","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/37239#issuecomment-1215345136","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/37239","id":1215345136,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5IcLHw","user":{"login":"rafaqz","id":2534009,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI1MzQwMDk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2534009?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz","html_url":"https://github.com/rafaqz","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-08-15T16:45:36Z","updated_at":"2022-08-15T16:45:36Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"This would be very useful to have for packages like DimensionalData.jll. {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/arviz-devs/ArviZ.jl/issues/191","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/arviz-devs/ArviZ.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/arviz-devs/ArviZ.jl/issues/191/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/arviz-devs/ArviZ.jl/issues/191/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/arviz-devs/ArviZ.jl/issues/191/events","html_url":"https://github.com/arviz-devs/ArviZ.jl/pull/191","id":1280998601,"node_id":"PR_kwDODP2Uvs46Ksgg","number":191,"title":"Reimplement Dataset and InferenceData using DimensionalData","user":{"login":"sethaxen","id":8673634,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjg2NzM2MzQ=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8673634?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/sethaxen","html_url":"https://github.com/sethaxen","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sethaxen/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sethaxen/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sethaxen/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sethaxen/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sethaxen/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sethaxen/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sethaxen/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sethaxen/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sethaxen/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2022-06-22T21:50:14Z","updated_at":"2022-06-23T10:09:04Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":true,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/arviz-devs/ArviZ.jl/pulls/191","html_url":"https://github.com/arviz-devs/ArviZ.jl/pull/191","diff_url":"https://github.com/arviz-devs/ArviZ.jl/pull/191.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/arviz-devs/ArviZ.jl/pull/191.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This PR reimplements `Dataset` to be a `DimensionalData.AbstractDimStack` that behaves identically to `DimensionalData.DimStack`, and `InferenceData` as a keyed collection of `Dataset`s. DimensionalData is the closest thing in the Julia ecosystem to an xarray replacement, in that it has a structured like `xarray.Dataset`. Its API is quite different from xarray's, so this is a major breaking change.

`InferenceData` and `Dataset` can be transparently converted to `arviz.InferenceData` and `xarray.Dataset` with no copying of data, so there is no appreciable loss in efficiency by having the storage be in Julia.

Fixes #128 ","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/arviz-devs/ArviZ.jl/issues/191/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/arviz-devs/ArviZ.jl/issues/191/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/arviz-devs/ArviZ.jl/issues/comments/1164224221","html_url":"https://github.com/arviz-devs/ArviZ.jl/pull/191#issuecomment-1164224221","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/arviz-devs/ArviZ.jl/issues/191","id":1164224221,"node_id":"IC_kwDODP2Uvs5FZKbd","user":{"login":"rafaqz","id":2534009,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI1MzQwMDk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2534009?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz","html_url":"https://github.com/rafaqz","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-06-23T10:09:04Z","updated_at":"2022-06-23T10:09:04Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"Probably you should manually use a `ForwardOrdered` `Categorical` lookup for your string lookups instead of `Sampled`. `AbsyractString` and `Symbol` lookups are normally detected as categorical but `PyObject` is being detected as `Sampled` here. Converting the PyObject to `String` may also be faster for lookups.

PR reimplements `Dataset` to be a `DimensionalData.AbstractDimStack` that behaves identically to `DimensionalData.DimStack`, and `InferenceData` as a keyed collection of `Dataset`s. DimensionalData is the closest thing in the Julia ecosystem to an xarray replacement, in that it has a structured like `xarray.Dataset`. Its API is quite different from xarray's, so this is a major breaking change.

`InferenceData` and `Dataset` can be transparently converted to `arviz.InferenceData` and `xarray.Dataset` with no copying of data, so there is no appreciable loss in efficiency by having the storage be in Julia.

Fixes #128 ","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/arviz-devs/ArviZ.jl/issues/191/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/arviz-devs/ArviZ.jl/issues/191/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/arviz-devs/ArviZ.jl/issues/comments/1164673354","html_url":"https://github.com/arviz-devs/ArviZ.jl/pull/191#issuecomment-1164673354","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/arviz-devs/ArviZ.jl/issues/191","id":1164673354,"node_id":"IC_kwDODP2Uvs5Fa4FK","user":{"login":"rafaqz","id":2534009,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI1MzQwMDk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2534009?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz","html_url":"https://github.com/rafaqz","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rafaqz/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-06-23T17:19:28Z","updated_at":"2022-06-23T17:19:28Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"What I mean is using `Dim{:chain}(NoIndex())` means there is no lookup for e.g. `At` or `Near` selectors to find, just a dimension name with regular array index 1:end. That seems to be what you want.

`@dim Chain` will create the `Chain` type.  The main gain of this is being able to use it as a wrapper more easily, not having to type the colon, and plot axes.

`@dim Chain IndependentDim` or `@dim Chain XDim` will put chain on the x axis in plots. `DependentDim` or `YDim` for the y axis.

You don't need to define your own abstract type unless you have some use for it.
